comment_content: Just finished watching the first two episode of this and the reasoning behind voting for Lauren was so silly.
reply_content:
​​​​​​​​@mimiad397 Her value was in the fact that she wasn't a strong competitor. To avoid being seen as a threat or angering everyone else enough to send them home, a good player would've never told anyone they had the money OR would've simply lied when everyone asked about her tasks as millionaire rather than admit she doesn't want to tell.

They all called Lauren's gameplay bad before the episode she got sent home. Her having bad gameplay is a plus for them b/c it means she's unlikely to catch them if they end up becoming the millionaire later down the line.

If I were a player, I'd have gotten rid of everybody who clocked Lauren as the millionaire, so I'd only be left w/ ppl easily fooled/manipulated if I later end up w/ the money. Having ppl good at spotting the millionaire only benefits you when you aren't the millionaire & that title could land on anyone at random anytime during the game, lessening their own chances of actually winning.

For Phil, voting for Sydney instead of Lauren would've been smarter b/c on top of Lauren being less likely to weed him out, when Lauren was proven wrong again (after having voted against every single person already previously sent home incorrectly) would have been most ppl's next target out of sheer frustration/annoyance. Getting her out just gave ppl more time to focus on him now that someone triggering their emotions was gone & at a time when he really needed to fly under the radar so he could move the money. Plus his vote wouldn't have broke the tie b/c it would have become 6-4 instead of 5-5 & there wouldn't have been that hint about the millionaire basically sending Lauren home.

Its similar.  But id say this is better - it mixes elements of The Mole and Traitors too.  Theres more for the secret millionaire to do with their tasks - so they have to be more careful in balancing bejng either too aggressive  in gameplay or too passive and risking losing the tasks and therefore getting rumbled.

My nitpick with the Traitors was always that there wasnt enough for a traitor to do that would lead to other players being able to successfully pick up on clues to identify them.

Here each task has direct consequence and action depending on how the millionaire plays.

Its been an engaging watch so far.
